---
title: "slides"
output:
  xaringan::moon_reader:
    css: ["default", "css/xaringan-themer.css"]
    self_contained: false
    lib_dir: libs
    nature:
      ratio: "16:9"
    seal: false
---

# STAT 436: Statistical Data Visualization

## Spring 2023

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
library(tidyverse)
library(knitr)
opts_chunk$set(
  echo = TRUE,
  message = FALSE,
  warning = FALSE,
  cache = TRUE,
  dpi = 200,
  fig.align = "center",
  out.width = 650,
  fig.height = 3,
  fig.width = 9
  )
th <- theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    panel.background = element_rect(fill = "#f7f7f7"),
    panel.border = element_rect(fill = NA, color = "#0c0c0c", size = 0.6),
    axis.text = element_text(size = 14),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 16),
    legend.position = "bottom"
  )
theme_set(th)
options(width = 100)
```

---


### Welcome!!

I'm excited to share this course with you this semester. There are few
activities that engage creative, quantitative, and critical thinking skills all
at once, but data visualization is one of them.

Nothing makes my day like a good data visualization, and I look forward to
seeing those that you create.

---

### Syllabus

* Learning outcomes
* Teaching team and office hours
* Assessments
* Grading

---

### Learning Outcomes

1.	Develop a vocabulary of visual encodings that support exploration of
geospatial, temporal, tree-structured, and network data, and demonstrate
facility implementing them using packages in the R programming language.
1.	Design dynamic queries that support interactive visualization of
heterogeneous data, and demonstrate facility implementing them using the shiny
package in the R programming language.
1.	Design effective visualizations to summarize the results of dimensionality
reduction and clustering algorithms.

---

### Learning Outcomes

1.	Use visual artifacts derived from complex statistical and machine learning
models to discuss the patterns they learn and mistakes they make.
1.	Evaluate strengths and weaknesses of visualizations in relation to the tasks
they support.

---


### Office Hours

Instructor: Kris Sankaran [ksankaran@wisc.edu]

TA: Margaret Turner

We will announce in-person and virtual office hours depending on the results of
this poll: https://go.wisc.edu/p5k1sm

---


### Assessments

* In-class exercises and discussions
* 4 Homework assignments
* 3 group project reports and 1 presentation
  - Teams of 3 - 5 people
* 1 midterm exam

---

### Grading

* In-Class Exercises: 15%
* Midterm: 20%
* Homework: 35%
* Group Project: 25%

---

### Class Resources

1. [Canvas page](https://canvas.wisc.edu/courses/346654/modules)
  - Links to all other resources
1. [Readings](https://github.com/krisrs1128/stat436_s23/raw/main/logistics/syllabus.docx) (Page 3)
1. [Piazza](https://canvas.wisc.edu/courses/346654/external_tools/65)
1. [Assignments](https://www.gradescope.com/courses/498858)
1. [Online notes](https://krisrs1128.github.io/stat436_s23/)
1. [Online recordings](https://mediaspace.wisconsin.edu/) (also linked in notes)
1. [Worked code examples](https://github.com/krisrs1128/stat436_s23/tree/main/activities/demos)

---

### Questions?

Also feel free to speak with me after class.

---

### Preparation

* For next lecture, bring your laptops
* Make sure you can run the [starter code](https://github.com/krisrs1128/stat436_s23/blob/main/activities/demos/demo1-1-starter.Rmd) for In-Class Demo [1-1]
  - I recommend using Rstudio
  - You can install the tidyverse package using
  
```{r, eval = FALSE}
install.packages("tidyverse") 
```

---

### Exercise 1-1

1. Introduce yourself to your neighbors. Share your name, degree program, and
one of: a recent hobby, favorite movie, favorite food. Share your chosen
datasets in groups of 2 - 3 people.

1. Submit a response to [Formulating Questions] on
[Gradescope](https://www.gradescope.com/courses/498858/assignments/2595893). The course entry code is WV68WB.

1. We will not grade the plot's quality -- we just want to get a sense of
everyone's starting point.

---

### Exercise 1-1

[Formulating Questions] Pick a dataset from
[TidyTuesday](https://github.com/rfordatascience/tidytuesday#datasets), [Data Is
Plural](https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1wZhPLMCHKJvwOkP4juclhjFgqIY8fQFMemwKL2c64vk/edit#gid=0),
[Kaggle Datasets](https://www.kaggle.com/datasets), [Google Dataset
Search](https://datasetsearch.research.google.com/), or your own personal
studies that you would like to visualize this semester. 

a. What makes you interested in this dataset? Please provide a link, so we can
use it to create future exercises.

b. Using any tools of your choice, make one example visualization from your
dataset. What question does it help to answer?

---

## January 26, 2023

1. Review Exercise 1 - 1
1. [In-Class Demo 1 - 1](https://github.com/krisrs1128/stat436_s23/blob/main/activities/demos/demo1-1-starter.Rmd)
1. [In-Class Exercise 1 - 2](https://www.gradescope.com/courses/498858/assignments/2603279)

---

### Announcements

1. We will accept responses to the Office Hours Poll
(https://go.wisc.edu/p5k1sm) until Friday at 5pm. Let us know your preferences!
1. Project Milestones 0 and 1 have been posted on Gradescope. Due February 6 and
13.
1. For in-class exercises, please submit a compiled PDF, HTML or DOCX file. We
will only need your original code in the HW submissions.

---

### Exercise Review

You can see responses at this link: https://go.wisc.edu/vp50pd

```{r}
responses <- read_csv("https://github.com/krisrs1128/stat436_s23/raw/main/data/exercise1-1-data.csv")
head(responses)
```


---

### Exercise Review

What types of topics seem to interest students in this class?

```{r}
responses %>%
  count(category) %>%
  arrange(-n)
```

---

### Exercise Review

```{r}
responses %>%
  count(category) %>%
  ggplot() +
  geom_col(aes(category, n))
```

---

### Exercise Review

```{r}
responses %>%
  count(category) %>%
  ggplot() +
  geom_col(aes(n, category))
```

---

### Exercise Review

```{r}
responses %>%
  count(category) %>%
  ggplot() +
  geom_col(aes(n, reorder(category, n)))
```

---

### Exercise Review

```{r, fig.height = 4, fig.width = 8, echo = FALSE}
responses %>%
  count(category) %>%
  ggplot() +
  geom_col(aes(n, reorder(category, n))) +
  scale_x_continuous(expand = c(0, 0, 0.1, 0)) +
  labs(x = "Count", y = "Topic")
```

---

### Exercise Review

```{r, fig.height = 4, fig.width = 8, eval = FALSE}
responses %>%
  count(category) %>%
  ggplot() +
  geom_col(aes(n, reorder(category, n))) +
  scale_x_continuous(expand = c(0, 0, 0.1, 0)) +
  labs(x = "Count", y = "Topic")
```


---

### Exercise Review

What types of visualization techniques and software to students seem most
familiar with?

```{r}
responses %>%
  count(visualization, package, defaults) %>%
  arrange(-n)
```


---

### Exercise Review

What types of visualization techniques and software to students seem most
familiar with?

```{r, fig.width = 8, fig.height = 4}
responses %>%
  count(visualization, package, defaults) %>%
  ggplot() +
  geom_col(aes(n, reorder(visualization, n, sum)))
```

---

### Exercise Review

What types of visualization techniques and software to students seem most
familiar with?

```{r, fig.width = 8, fig.height = 4, echo = FALSE}
responses %>%
  count(visualization, package, defaults) %>%
  ggplot() +
  geom_col(aes(n, reorder(visualization, n, sum), fill = defaults)) +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  labs(x = "Count", y = "Vis Type", fill = "Used Defaults?") +
  scale_x_continuous(expand = c(0, 0, 0.1, 0)) +
  theme(legend.text = element_text(size = 12))
```

---

### Exercise Review

What types of visualization techniques and software to students seem most
familiar with?

```{r, fig.width = 8, fig.height = 4, eval = FALSE}
responses %>%
  count(visualization, package, defaults) %>%
  ggplot() +
  geom_col(aes(n, reorder(visualization, n, sum), fill = defaults)) +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  labs(x = "Count", y = "Vis Type", fill = "Used Defaults?") +
  scale_x_continuous(expand = c(0, 0, 0.1, 0)) +
  theme(legend.text = element_text(size = 12))
```

---


### Exercise Review

What types of visualization techniques and software to students seem most
familiar with?

```{r, fig.width = 8, fig.height = 6, echo = FALSE}
responses %>%
  count(visualization, package, defaults) %>%
  ggplot() +
  geom_col(aes(n, reorder(visualization, n), fill = defaults)) +
  facet_grid(reorder(package, n, max, decreasing = TRUE) ~ ., scales = "free_y", space = "free_y") +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  labs(x = "Count", y = "Vis Type", fill = "Used Defaults?") +
  scale_x_continuous(expand = c(0, 0, 0.1, 0)) +
  theme(
    legend.text = element_text(size = 12),
    strip.text.y = element_text(angle = 0, size = 14)
    )
```

---

### [In-Class Demo 1-1](https://github.com/krisrs1128/stat436_s23/blob/main/activities/demos/demo1-1-starter.Rmd)


The data below measures properties of various Antarctic penguins.

```{r, echo = TRUE}
library(tidyverse)
theme_set(theme_bw())
penguins <- read_csv("https://uwmadison.box.com/shared/static/ijh7iipc9ect1jf0z8qa2n3j7dgem1gh.csv")
```

Create a single plot that makes it easy to answer both of these questions, (i)
How is bill length related to bill depth within and across species? (ii) On
which islands are which species found?

---

### Exercise 1-2

Response to *one* of the following questions in the exercise sheet:

* [Critique - Infographic]
* [Critique - Science Paper I]
* [Critique - Science Paper II]

---

## January 31, 2023

1. Review Exercise 1 - 2
1. [In-Class Demo 2 - 1](https://go.wisc.edu/06p2bd)
1. [In-Class Exercise 2 - 1](https://go.wisc.edu/2g3c9k)

---

### Announcements

1. Office Hours are set (also on the syllabus)
  - Prof. Sankaran: 3:45 - 5pm Tuesdays (MSC 7225C)
  - Margaret: 2 - 3pm Fridays (MSC 1274)
1. For future In-Class exercise submissions, we would prefer HTML format (but it
is not required).
1. Corrections on HW1 [Problem 1].

---

### Exercise Review

.center[
  <img src="figure/info-food-waste.png" width=700/>
]

---

### Exercise Review

* What is the meaning of these scales? Are 20 million slices of bread really
comparable to 290,000 kg of poultry? What about CO2 equivalents?
* A barchart would make quantitative comparisons much easier, especially for
smaller circles
* Bright pink background...
* The text is sometimes quite small
* Are apples really vegetables??
* The icons are nice

---

### Exercise Review

.center[
 <img src="figure/mosquito-predators.jpeg" width=600/>
]

---

### Exercise Review

* This is actually quite elegant, thought the visualization form is not that
standard
* Several responses commented that additional labeling or color would help. I
would caution that these additions should not come at the cost of the current
information density.
* In an interactive version, it would be nice to be able to filter by predation
type

---

### Exercise Review

.center[
 <img src="figure/elife_sunflower.jpeg" width=500/>
]

---

### Exercise Review

* This is a very dense visualization, appropriate for a scientific venue, but
requiring significant time to parse (though if a lot is learned through the
process, this need not be a bad thing)
* Having the scatterplot icons look like the properties of the sunflowers they
are studying was a brilliant idea.
* The linking of the same color across many subplots is a good practice.
However, the circle sizes are not consistent between (a) and (b).
* Some of the text makes the figure unnecessarily busy. E.g., do we really need
to know latitude and longitude?

---

### In-Class Demo [2-1]

* We will work through [Plant Growth Experiment] in the exercises sheet.
* Starter code is available here: https://go.wisc.edu/06p2bd

---

### In-Class Exercise [2 - 1]

* [Submit](https://go.wisc.edu/2g3c9k) an answer to [Code Analysis] from the exercises sheet.

[Code Analysis] Pick one code example from this week’s lecture notes. Provide a
3 - 4 sentence summary of the overall implementation strategy. Then, add
comments every 10 - 15 lines describing what each section of code is doing. If
there are parts that you do not understand, add a ??? next to it (we will review
these in the next lecture).

---

## February 2, 2023

1. Review Exercise 2 - 1
1. [In-Class Demo 2 - 2](https://go.wisc.edu/504ci4)
1. [In-Class Exercise 2 - 2](https://go.wisc.edu/ni1n8e)

---

### Exercise Review

Several students were confused about `pivot_fun` in the Pivoting notes

```{r}
pivot_fun <- function(x, value_column = "cases") {
  x %>%
    pivot_longer(c(`1999`, `2000`), names_to = "year", values_to = value_column)
}
```

---

### Exercise Review

To understand this, first consider the inputs and outputs of the ordinary
`pivot_longer` function.

```{r}
table4a
```

---

### Exercise Review

To understand this, first consider the inputs and outputs of the ordinary
`pivot_longer` function.

```{r}
table4a %>%
  pivot_longer(c(`1999`, `2000`))
```

---

### Exercise Review

To understand this, first consider the inputs and outputs of the ordinary
`pivot_longer` function.

```{r}
table4a %>%
  pivot_longer(c(`1999`, `2000`), names_to = "year", values_to = "cases")
```

---

### Exercise Review

Next, note that the transformations for the two example tables were very similar
to one another. The only thing that changed is the `values_to` argument.

```{r}
table4b %>%
  pivot_longer(c(`1999`, `2000`), names_to = "year", values_to = "population")
```

---

### Exercise Review

Whenever I find myself copying and pasting code, I ask whether it might be
better to write a function instead. Advantages:

* If I ever need to change the code, I only need to change it in one place.
* Overall, there would be fewer lines of code (easier for someone new to read)
* Creates an abstraction (makes higher-level logic clearer)

---

### Exercise Review

Since the two pivot commands were so similar, I created a function with an
argument for the only piece that varied.

```{r}
pivot_fun <- function(x, value_column = "cases") {
  x %>%
    pivot_longer(c(`1999`, `2000`), names_to = "year", values_to = value_column)
}
```

---

### Exercise Review

.pull-left[
```{r}
pivot_fun(table4a, "cases")
```
]

.pull-right[
```{r}
pivot_fun(table4b, "population")
```
]

---

### Exercise Review

There was a question about compound figures (this is only in next week's
notes):

```{r, include = FALSE}
library(patchwork)
athletes =
  read_csv("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/krisrs1128/stat436_s23/main/data/athletes.csv") %>%
#filter out "basketball", "field", "rowing", "swimming", "tennis", "track (400m)" in the sports column
   filter(sport %in% c("basketball", "field", "rowing", "swimming", "tennis", "track (400m)")) %>%
#redefine the "m" and "f" to male and female
   mutate(sex = recode(sex, "m" = "male", "f" = "female"))

 p <- list()
 p[["bar"]] <- ggplot(count(athletes, sex)) + #count observations of athletes of different sex 
   geom_bar(aes(sex, n, fill = sex), stat = "identity") + #set x, y, and different colors to fill the bar depending on sex
   scale_y_continuous(expand = c(0, 0)) + #absolute
   scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set1") + #customize colors
   labs(y = "number") # set name of y axis
 
 p[["scatter"]] <- ggplot(athletes) +
   geom_point(aes(rcc, wcc, col = sex)) + # set x,y, and colors of different sex
   scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set1") + #customize colors
   theme(legend.position = "none") + #no legend
   labs(x = "RBC count", y = "WBC Count") #set names of x and y axis
 
 p[["box"]] <- ggplot(athletes) +
   geom_boxplot(aes(sport, pcBfat, col = sex, fill = sex), alpha = 0.5) +
   scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set1") + #customize colors
   scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set1") + #fill box with colors
   theme(legend.position = "none") + #no legend
   labs(y = "% body fat", x = NULL) #set names of x and y axis


p[["bar"]] <- p[["bar"]] + ggtitle("a")
p[["scatter"]] <- p[["scatter"]] + ggtitle("b")
p[["box"]] <- p[["box"]] + ggtitle("c")
```

```{r, fig.height = 4, fig.width = 8}
 (p[["bar"]] + p[["scatter"]]) / p[["box"]] +
   plot_layout(guides = "collect") +
   plot_annotation(theme = theme(legend.position = "bottom", title = element_text(size = 10)))
```

---

### Exercise Review

The difference in the figures is small -- it just moves the legend to the bottom
of the overall figure.

```{r, fig.height = 4, fig.width = 8}
 (p[["bar"]] + p[["scatter"]]) / p[["box"]] +
   plot_annotation(theme = theme(legend.position = "bottom", title = element_text(size = 10)))
```

---

### Miscellaneous Comments

* I encourage you to remove extraneous package loading messages from your
exercise and homework solutions (give example)
* Pipe syntax seems to be a new concept. The main idea is to avoid storing
intermediate computations.

.pull-left[
```{r, eval = FALSE}
df2 <- f(df1)
df3 <- g(df2)
```
]

.pull-right[
```{r, eval = FALSE}
df3 <- df1 %>%
  f() %>%
  g()
```
]




---

### In-Class Demo [2-2]

* We will work through [Olympics Derivations] in the exercises sheet.
* Starter code is available here: https://go.wisc.edu/504ci4

---

### In-Class Exercise [2 - 2]

* Work with your project team (or if you do not have one, your neighbors in
class), to complete the sections of this google doc: https://go.wisc.edu/ni1n8e
* You do not need to submit anything to gradescope.

---

## February 7, 2023

1. In-Class Discussion [3-1]
1. In-Class Demo [3-1]
1. In-Class Exercise [3-1]

---

### Announcements

* Thank you for your Project Milestone 0 submissions!
* Project Milestone 1 is due on February 13.
* Homework 1 is due on February 19. I encourage you to start early, since we
have covered the technical material needed to solve the problems.

---

### In-Class Discussion [3-1]

* At https://go.wisc.edu/i83g3i, response to *one* of the two prompt options.
* The first prompt is related to the code demo we will work through. The second
is related to the reading and notes for the week.
* Discuss and answer the prompts with your project group. Include your project
group number next to  your answer.

---

### In-Class Demo [3-1]

* We will work through [Pokemon] in the exercises sheet.
* Starter code is available here: https://go.wisc.edu/96tvyn

---

### In-Class Exercise [3-1]

* Submit [https://go.wisc.edu/40q009](https://go.wisc.edu/40q009) an answer to [Ikea Faceting] from the
exercises sheet. Provide a brief explanation for your selection.
* We only need one submission per project group -- make sure to name all your
team members in the submission.
* Note: This is an example of a past midterm problem.

---

## February 9, 2023

1. Exercise Review
1. In-Class Demo [3-2]
1. In-Class Exercise [3-2]

---

## February 9, 2023

* Project Milestone 1 is due on February 13.
* Homework 1 is due on February 19. I encourage you to start early, since we
have covered the technical material needed to solve the problems.
* Remember that there are recordings associated with each of the lecture notes.
* During our demos, I will focus on explaining only those steps in the posted
solutions. If you would like to discuss other approaches / refinements, you can
ask me during the in-class exercise portions of the class.

---

### Exercise Review

Here is the original histogram, without any faceting by category.

```{r}
ikea <- read_csv("https://uwmadison.box.com/shared/static/iat31h1wjg7abhd2889cput7k264bdzd.csv") %>%
          filter(category %in% c("Tables", "Bookcases", "Beds", "Cabinets", "Sofas", "Wardrobes"))
p <- ggplot(ikea) +
  geom_histogram(aes(log(price)))
p
```

---

### Exercise Review (Part (a))

.pull-left[
* `facet_grid` controls whether we explicitly arrange facets along rows or columns.
* If we place the variable before the `~`, it arranges them along rows.
]

.pull-right[
```{r, fig.height = 3.9, fig.width = 4}
p + facet_grid(category ~ .)
```
]

---

### Exercise Review (Part (b))

.pull-left[
* The remaining plots all use `facet_wrap`
* By default, the axes all use the same scale, which rules out (ii)
* By default, the facets are sorted alphabetically, which rules out (iv)
]

.pull-right[
```{r, fig.width = 6.5, fig.height = 4}
p + facet_wrap(~ category)
```
]

---

### Exercise Review (Part (c))

The only plot with different $y$-axes per facet is plot (iii).

```{r, fig.width = 6.5, fig.height = 4}
p + facet_wrap(~ category, scales = "free_y")
```

---

### Exercise Review (Part (d))

`axes = "separate"` is not an actual option in `facet_grid`

```{r, eval = FALSE}
p + facet_wrap(~ category, axes = "separate")
```

---

### Exercise Review (Part (e))

Looking at the $x$-axis values of plot (iv), we can see that the facets are
sorted from those with the lowest to those with the highest average price.

```{r, fig.width = 6.5, fig.height = 4, out.width = 600}
p + facet_wrap(~ reorder(category, price))
```

---

### In-Class Demo

* Before we work through the demo together, discuss your initial attempt here: https://go.wisc.edu/6bw633
* We will work through [Multiple Weather Variables] in the exercises sheet.
* Starter code is available here: https://go.wisc.edu/90oj6g

---

### In-Class Exercise

* [Submit](https://go.wisc.edu/2sag6l) an answer to [Faceting Rules of Thumb] from the
exercises sheet. Provide a brief explanation for your selection.
* We only need one submission per project group -- make sure to name all your
team members in the submission.

---

## February 14, 2023

1. Exercise Review
1. In-Class Discussion [4-1]
1. In-Class Demo [4-1]
1. In-Class Exercise [4-1]

---

## Announcements

* Homework 1 is due on February 19.
* The Project Milestones 2 and 3 description have been released.
* I will post the in-class demo solutions only on the [github page](https://github.com/krisrs1128/stat436_s23/tree/main/activities/demos).

---

### Exercise Review

_Whenever possible, each panel in a faceted plot should be made to have its own $y$-axis scale._

False: Readers generally assume the facets have the same axes scales. Free
scales should only be provided if the resulting figure would not be legible
otherwise. In this case, the caption should highlight the fact that the scales
are not the same across facets.

---

### Exercise Review

_When using the `patchwork` package, two plots `p1` and `p2` can be placed side-by-side using `p1 / p2`._

False: `/` places panels on top of one another. To place them side by side, we
can use `+`.

---

### Exercise Review

_When using the `patchwork` package, the `plot_layout` function can be used to collect legends._


True: This can be done by calling `plot_layout(guides = "collect")`.

---

### Exercise Review

_Relative to interactively filtered displays, faceted plots are less taxing on the reader's memory._

True: Faceting requires more space, but it allows comparisons to be made without
having to recall the previous interactive view. Tamara Munzner calls this
principle, "Eyes beat memory."

---

### Discussion [4 - 1]

* This discussion is a warm-up to interactive visualization.
* Respond to the prompt at https://go.wisc.edu/fhzrx4
* Discuss and answer the prompts with your project group. Include your project
group number next to  your answer.

---

### In-Class Demo [4 - 1]

* We will work through [California Wildfires] in the exercises sheet.
* Starter code is available here: https://go.wisc.edu/5586t7

---

### In-Class Exercise [4 - 1]

* Submit an answer (https://go.wisc.edu/bzu64h) to [Name App Bugs, Parts a - c]
from the exercises sheet.
* You may discuss with others, but prepare and submit an individual response.

---

## February 16, 2023

1. Exercise Review
1. In-Class Discussion [4 - 2]
1. In-Class Demos [4 - 2 - 1] and [4 - 2 - 2]

---
### Announcements

* Homework 1 is due on February 19.
  - No In-Class Exercise today
* The Project Milestones 2 and 3 descriptions have been released.

---

### Exercise Review (Part (a))

We cannot refer to `input$name` outside of a `render` or `reactive` environment.

```{r, eval = FALSE}
ui <- fluidPage(
  titlePanel("Hello!"),
  textInput("name", "Enter your name"),
  textOutput("printed_name")
)

server <- function(input, output) {
  input$name <- paste0("Welcome to shiny, ", input$name, "!")
  output$printed_names <- renderText({ input$name })
}
```

---

### Exercise Review (Part (b))

There is a mismatch between the `textOutput` and `renderText` elements. The
names need to match exactly for the `ui` and `server` elements to be properly
associated.

```{r, eval = FALSE}
ui <- fluidPage(
  titlePanel("Hello!"),
  textInput("name", "Enter your name"),
  textOutput("printed_name")
)
    
server <- function(input, output) {
  output$printed_names <- renderText({
    paste0("Welcome to shiny, ", input$name, "!")
  })
}
```    

---

### Exercise Review (Part (c))

There is a mismatch in the `ui` and `server` element for `printed_name`. If we
have a `textOutput` in `ui`, it needs to be matched with a `renderText`, not a
`renderDataTable`.

```{r, eval = FALSE}
ui <- fluidPage(
  titlePanel("Hello!"),
  textInput("name", "Enter your name"),
  textOutput("printed_name")
)
    
server <- function(input, output) {
  output$printed_name <- renderDataTable({
    paste0("Welcome to shiny, ", input$name, "!")
  })
}
```

---

### In-Class Demo [4 - 2 - 2]

* Before we work through the demo together, discuss your initial attempt here: https://go.wisc.edu/g0u74j
* We will work through [Interactive German Traffic] in the exercises sheet.
* Starter code is available here: https://go.wisc.edu/2yu4ht

---

### In-Class Discussion [4 - 2]

* Respond to the prompt at https://go.wisc.edu/g0u74j . This is designed to
help you consolidate the last week's introduction to Shiny.
* Discuss and answer the prompts with your project group. Include your project
group number next to  your answer.

---

### In-Class Demo [4 - 2 - 1]

* We will work through [Improving an app] in the exercises sheet.
* Starter code is available here: https://go.wisc.edu/w0r37e

---

## February 21, 2023

1. Discussion Review
1. In-Class Demo [5 - 1]
1. In-Class Discussion [5 - 1]
1. In-Class Exercise [5 - 1]

---

### Discussion Review

A few main themes came up in Thursday's discussion (https://go.wisc.edu/g0u74j)

* Making sense of Shiny-specific vocabulary and syntax
* Which Shiny components should we use when? What considerations guide the
choice?
* How to debug Shiny apps?

---

### Shiny-specific Syntax

What is the difference between `ui` and `server`?

* The `ui` defines what the user sees and can interact with.
* The `server` defines how the visualization responds to inputs.

---

### Shiny-specific Syntax

What is the difference between `observe` and `reactive`?

From Mastering Shiny, Section 3.6,

> [S]ometimes you need to reach outside of the app and cause side-effects to
happen elsewhere in the world. This might be saving a file to a shared network
drive, sending data to a web API, updating a database, or (most commonly)
printing a debugging message to the console. These actions don’t affect how your
app looks, so you shouldn’t use an output and a render function. Instead you
need to use an observer.

---

### Which Components to Use When?

* For choosing between different inputs and outputs, the [Shiny Cheat
Sheet](https://shiny.rstudio.com/images/shiny-cheatsheet.pdf) is helpful. It
shows screenshots of each type of input / output.
* Many authors have implemented inputs / outputs beyond those available in the main Shiny package. E.g., there are specialized widgets in the [`shinyWidgets`](https://shinyapps.dreamrs.fr/shinyWidgets/) package

---

### Which Components to Use When?

* For more complex displays, you may want to use `sidebarLayout`, `tabsetPanel`,
or `navbarPage`
* We will practice with alternatives during the Thursday demo

---

### Debugging Shiny Apps

In addition to printing to the R console or inserting a `browser()` call, you
can create UI elements that print variables of interest.
  * `verbatimOutput` will print arbitrary R objects to the shiny UI


```{r, eval = FALSE}
server <- function(input, output) {
  output$debug <- verbatimOutput({
    # intermediate data structure 
  })
}
```

---

### Debugging Shiny Apps

A general principle for debugging: If you are very stuck, it helps to simplify
the problem. Pare away parts of the code that seem unrelated to the bug until
you arrive at a minimal example that reproduces the same buggy behavior. In the
process, you may discover the solution, and even if not, you will have a version
of the code that others can easily give feedback on.

---

### In-Class Demo [5 - 1]

* We will give a new solution to part (c) of [California Wildfires] in the
exercise sheet, but this time using graphical queries.
* Starter code is available here: https://go.wisc.edu/oldyzu

---

### In-Class Discussion [5 - 1]

Document: https://go.wisc.edu/2ac0fg

Prompt: Right now, the dotplot responds to the brushed histogram. However,

  * The dotplot does not allow any user inputs.
  * The histogram does not reflect year or user selections.

Imagine someone came to your statistical consulting hours with this application.
They want your expertise to improve the app so that it conveys more information.
How would you approach this? What might be some first steps towards
implementation?

---

### In-Class Exercise [5 - 1]

We will work through the [Code Analysis] exercise again, but for material from
Shiny. https://go.wisc.edu/3pr7v5

[Code Analysis] Pick one code example from this week’s lecture notes. Provide a
3 - 4 sentence summary of the overall implementation strategy. Then, add
comments every 10 - 15 lines describing what each section of code is doing. If
there are parts that you do not understand, add a ??? next to it (we will review
these in the next lecture).

---

## February 23, 2023

1. Exercise Review
1. In-Class Demo [5 - 2]
1. In-Class Discussion [5 - 2]
1. In-Class Exercise [5 - 2]

---

### Announcements

* We are done with the first course module (Fundamentals of Data Visualization).
* There is a new Gradescope item where you can (optionally) share comments about
your teammate's contributions to Project Milestone 1.
* Important Dates: HW2 (March 13), Midterm 2 (March 23), Project Milestone 2
(March 27)

---

### Exercise Review

A few people had questions about the `reset_selection()` function in ["Graphical Queries - Brush Events."](https://krisrs1128.github.io/stat436_s23/posts/2022-12-27-week05-02/)

```{r, eval = FALSE}
 reset_selection <- function(x, brush) {
   brushedPoints(x, brush, allRows = TRUE)$selected_
 }

...

server <- function(input, output) {
 selected <- reactiveVal(rep(TRUE, nrow(mtcars)))
 observeEvent(
   input$plot_brush,
   selected(reset_selection(mtcars, input$plot_brush))
 )
```

---

### Exercise Review

This was defined just to simplify the server code. We could have alternatively
written the `brushedPoints()` call within the server.

```{r, eval = FALSE}
server <- function(input, output) {
 selected <- reactiveVal(rep(TRUE, nrow(mtcars)))
 observeEvent(
   input$plot_brush, {
     new_selection <- brushedPoints(mtcars, input$plot_brush, allRows = TRUE)$selected_
     selected(new_selection)
   }
 )
```

---

### Exercise Review

The `column()` function is used to place two `plotOutput`s side-by-side within a
single `fluidRow()`. The first argument specifies the relative width (12 is the
full screen width, 1 is 1/12 of the screen width).

```{r, eval = FALSE}
ui <- fluidPage(
  fluidRow(
    column(6, plotOutput("scatter1", brush = "plot_brush")),
    column(6, plotOutput("scatter2", brush = "plot_brush"))
```

---

### Exercise Review

In the ["Introduction to Reactivity"](https://krisrs1128.github.io/stat436_s23/posts/2022-12-27-week04-02/) notes, we need to create a new sample with
every user interaction because we want to be able to compute test results
on-the-fly.

```{r eval = FALSE}
output$histogram <- renderPlot(
  histogram_fun(generate_data(input$n, input$mean1, input$mean2, input$sigma))
) # ??? Why is a new dataset generated for each rendering call? What is the point of creating the samples variable?
```

---

### Exercise Review

This is unrelated to Shiny, but we wanted to include `NA` as a genre in the
visualization's legend. By default, `ggplot2` would have dropped those movies.

```{r, eval = FALSE}
movies <- read_csv("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/krisrs1128/stat479_s22/main/_posts/2022-02-10-week04-03/apps/data/movies.csv") %>% #import csv dataset
 mutate(
   date = as_date(Release_Date, format = "%b %d %Y"),
   year = year(date), #extract the year from the date
   Major_Genre = fct_explicit_na(Major_Genre), #??? why should we make the NA values explicit, can't we just delete it or filling a new value?
```

---

### Exercise Review

* We had questions about the use of `.data[[var_name]]` and `aes_string()`.
* These are both ways to let variables be passed into `ggplot2`'s aesthetic
mapping. It is useful whenever we use `ggplot` within functions.

```{r eval = FALSE}
# Doesn't work
penguins <- read_csv("https://uwmadison.box.com/shared/static/ijh7iipc9ect1jf0z8qa2n3j7dgem1gh.csv")

var_name <- "bill_length_mm"
ggplot(penguins) +
  geom_histogram(aes(var_name))
```

---

### Exercise Review

* We had questions about the use of `.data[[var_name]]` and `aes_string()`.
* These are both ways to let variables be passed into `ggplot2`'s aesthetic
mapping. It is useful whenever we use `ggplot` within functions.

```{r}
# Works
var_name <- "bill_length_mm"
ggplot(penguins) +
  geom_histogram(aes_string(var_name))
```

---

### Exercise Review

* We had questions about the use of `.data[[var_name]]` and `aes_string()`.
* These are both ways to let variables be passed into `ggplot2`'s aesthetic
mapping. It is useful whenever we use `ggplot` within functions.

```{r}
# Works
var_name <- "bill_length_mm"
ggplot(penguins) +
  geom_histogram(aes(.data[[var_name]]))
```

---

### In-Class Demo [5 - 2]

* We will work through [Customizing a Shiny App] in the exercise sheet.
* Before we work through this together, try your best to improve / customize our
earlier traffic Shiny app: https://go.wisc.edu/f05xr3
* Starter code is available here: https://go.wisc.edu/1fzadf

---

### In-Class Exercise [5 - 2]

With your project group, prepare a concept map to summarize the last week of
reading material. An effective submission will include a thorough coverage of
both abstract concepts and practical examples, summarized concisely into short
phrases. It will also include well-justified links between nodes, with text
explanations for edges whose interpretation might not be obvious at first.

* Submission: https://go.wisc.edu/fw0i3n
* Optional: Share your concept map in Piazza.

---

## February 28, 2023

1. In-Class Exercise [6 - 1]
1. In-Class Discussion [6 - 1]
1. In-Class Demo [6 - 1]

---

### Announcements

* We've left comments on some of the Discussion [5 - 2] notes
* Please make sure your group members are all listed in your Gradescope group
submissions. You should be able to set your group members on the submission
page.
* Important Dates: HW2 (March 13), Midterm 2 (March 23), Project Milestone 2
(March 27)

---

### Module 2 [Visualizing Structured Data]

Over the next three weeks, we'll study techniques to visualization time series,
geospatial data, and trees / networks.

.pull-left[
<img src="https://krisrs1128.github.io/stat436_s23/posts/2022-12-27-week06-02/2022-12-27-week06-02_files/figure-html5/unnamed-chunk-2-2.png"/>
]
.pull-right[
<img src="https://krisrs1128.github.io/stat436_s23/posts/2022-12-27-week08-02/2022-12-27-week08-02_files/figure-html5/unnamed-chunk-3-1.png"/>
]

---

### Today

By the end of the class, you should be able to...

* Construct and visualize a `tsibble` time series object
* Discuss the presence of trend, seasonal, or cyclic structure in a time series

---

### In-Class Exercise [6 - 1]

* I will pause at a few points during the demo today, and I want you to guess
what the next step in the analysis is.
* At the end of the demo, you can summarize your guesses and submit them to
Gradescope: https://go.wisc.edu/rc7li7
* You should be able to make your submission before the end of class.

---

### In-Class Demo: Expectations

* Take out your laptops, follow-along, and ask questions (raise your hand, or comment on google doc)
* You and I will both run into bugs -- this is a normal part of the process
* We will try to balance conceptual sketches with practical implementation

---

### In-Class Demo [6 - 1]

* We will work through as much of [Global Carbon Footprint Network] as we can.
Before we do this, take a look at the data with  your group and respond to part
(a): https://go.wisc.edu/wi625o
* Starter code is available here: https://go.wisc.edu/98vpl5

---

## March 2, 2023

1. In-Class Discussion [6 - 2]
1. In-Class Demo [6 - 2]
1. In-Class Exercise [6 - 2]

---

### Announcements

---

### In-Class Demo [6 - 2]

* We will work through [Global Carbon Footprint II].
* Before we do this, work with your neighbors to brainstorm a response to part
(b). Summarize your discussion: https://go.wisc.edu/k96khu
* Starter code is available here: https://go.wisc.edu/k99j43

---

### In-Class Exercise [6 - 2]

* Work through parts (a - b) in the [Spotify Time Series I] problem on the
exercise sheet: https://go.wisc.edu/6hzcz2
* You can discuss this with your neighbors, but make your submission
individually.
* You will get full credit if you make an honest attempt -- if you become stuck
at some step, describe the difficulty that you encountered.

---

## March 7, 2023

---

## March 9, 2023

---

## March 21, 2023

---

## March 23, 2023
